serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dry serving of uncooked pasta is approximately 80g, which yields roughly 180–200g of cooked pasta after boiling.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, dry, enriched — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20420)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for uncooked pasta (cr_id F05070013). HPB values used as primary for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol. USDA SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 49 for white pasta (spaghetti, boiled) is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 international GI tables (Am J Clin Nutr 2008;88:1981–2006). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 49 × 59.7 / 100 ≈ 29.3, based on 80g dry serving yielding ~74.67g carbohydrate × 0.80 available fraction.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6mg/100g) with beneficial potassium (223mg/100g) and fibre (3.2g/100g) supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Uncooked pasta is a low-sodium, potassium-containing staple suitable for hypertension management; prepare without added salt and pair with low-sodium vegetable-based sauces to preserve the cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high carbohydrate content (74.67g/100g uncooked) with moderate fibre (3.2g/100g); GI of 49 (low) applies to cooked pasta but the raw carbohydrate density demands strict portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Limit dry pasta to 60–80g per serving (yielding ~150–180g cooked), cook al dente to preserve the low GI, and choose wholemeal or legume-based pasta varieties to further reduce postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.28g/100g), negligible cholesterol (0mg/100g), and low sodium (6mg/100g) with moderate dietary fibre (3.2g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain uncooked pasta has a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile; pair with olive oil, vegetables, and lean protein rather than cream- or meat-based sauces to maintain a heart-healthy meal pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate load (74.67g/100g uncooked) with limited anti-inflammatory micronutrients; lacks omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols from the RA-beneficial food list
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Choose wholemeal pasta (dietary fibre >6g/100g uncooked) and pair with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as olive oil, tomatoes, and garlic to reduce glycaemic-load-mediated inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta, uncooked (dry pasta) is a staple grain product made from durum wheat semolina and water, extruded or rolled into various shapes — including spaghetti, penne, fusilli, and macaroni — and dried to a firm, pale-yellow form. Per 100g dry weight it provides 371 kcal, 13.04g protein, 1.51g fat (of which 0.28g saturated), and 74.67g carbohydrate with 3.2g dietary fibre and only 6mg sodium. When boiled in water, dry pasta approximately doubles in weight, reducing the effective carbohydrate density to roughly 25–30g per 100g cooked. The glycemic index of cooked white pasta is approximately 49 (low GI), particularly when cooked al dente, due to the compact starch matrix of durum wheat that slows enzymatic digestion. For hypertension, plain pasta is an excellent low-sodium staple within the DASH dietary pattern. For type 2 diabetes, portion control is critical given the high dry-weight carbohydrate content; wholemeal variants with higher fibre are preferred. For cardiovascular health, the negligible saturated fat and cholesterol make pasta a heart-friendly base when paired with vegetable-rich, low-fat sauces. Pasta is a cornerstone of Italian and broader Western cuisine, widely consumed globally and available in enriched, whole-wheat, legume-based, and gluten-free formulations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in uncooked pasta?
How much pasta is one serving?
Dry pasta vs cooked pasta calories

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05070013, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20420) for dry enriched pasta, which closely matches the HPB values. GI value of 49 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for white pasta cooked al dente.
notes: All per_100g values are for uncooked (dry) pasta. Cooked pasta per 100g contains approximately 25–30g carbohydrate due to water absorption during boiling. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources — pasta made from durum wheat semolina and water contains no animal-derived cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for plain dry pasta per USDA SR Legacy). Vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0 (confirmed absent in plain durum wheat pasta per USDA SR Legacy). Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed absent — no animal-derived ingredients). Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.17), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.56), and all vitamin/mineral values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 20420 as the closest approved equivalent. The GI value of 49 applies to cooked pasta; the glycemic load per serving is calculated on an 80g dry serving basis. The dietary_tag 'high_fibre' in the input is borderline — 3.2g/100g uncooked meets the Singapore HPB threshold of ≥3g/100g for a fibre claim on a dry product; this tag is retained with caution.
